stage: Stage 1: Initial Disputes - Direct to Bureaus
title: Basic Dispute - Inaccurate Account
when_to_use:
• You have at least one negative item on your credit report that you believe is factually inaccurate.
• Specific account information — such as the reported balance, payment status, account dates (date opened, date of last activity, date of first delinquency), or account ownership — is wrong.
• The item belongs to someone else (wrong consumer, mixed file, or identity-confusion) or is appearing as a duplicate of another tradeline already on your report.
• You are initiating the formal dispute process for the very first time regarding this specific item with this specific bureau.
• You have NOT yet exhausted your standard FCRA §611 dispute right; you must use this letter BEFORE you can demand the Method of Verification (Letter 2) or escalate to a Round 3 demand (Letter 46).
• You want to lock in the 30-day statutory investigation clock by sending a properly formatted, certified-mail dispute that the bureau cannot dismiss as "frivolous" under §611(a)(3).
• You are building a clean, court-admissible paper trail that can later support a willful-noncompliance claim under FCRA §616 (15 U.S.C. § 1681n) if the bureau refuses to correct the error.
• Use this letter FIRST, every time, for any factual inaccuracy on a tradeline. It is the foundation of every Stage 1 escalation chain in this library.
why_it_works:
• It triggers the bureau's mandatory reinvestigation duty under FCRA §611(a)(1)(A) (15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(1)(A)). Once the bureau receives a written dispute from a consumer regarding the completeness or accuracy of any item, it MUST conduct a reasonable reinvestigation to determine whether the disputed information is inaccurate, and must complete that reinvestigation within thirty (30) days of receipt.
• It puts the furnisher on notice. The bureau is required under FCRA §611(a)(2) (15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(2)) to forward the dispute, along with all relevant information you submitted, to the furnisher of the disputed information within five business days. Once the furnisher receives the notice of dispute, the furnisher's own duty under FCRA §623(b) (15 U.S.C. § 1681s-2(b)) is triggered — they must conduct an investigation, review the consumer's evidence, and report back to the bureau.
• It targets the furnisher's accuracy obligation under FCRA §623(a)(1)(A) (15 U.S.C. § 1681s-2(a)(1)(A)), which prohibits a furnisher from reporting information that the furnisher knows or has reasonable cause to believe is inaccurate. Once the furnisher is on notice and cannot verify accuracy, continued reporting becomes legally indefensible.
• It forces the bureau to satisfy the "maximum possible accuracy" standard of FCRA §607(b) (15 U.S.C. § 1681e(b)). Federal courts have held that this standard requires more than a mechanical electronic match — it requires reasonable procedures designed to assure that the data published is accurate as to the specific consumer.
• Properly drafted, it cannot be brushed off as "frivolous or irrelevant" under FCRA §611(a)(3) (15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(3)). By identifying the specific account, the specific data point, and the specific reason the data is wrong, it foreclosed the bureau's most common excuse for refusing to investigate.
• It captures the 30-day clock with certified-mail evidence. The signed return-receipt card is the date-stamped trigger for the §611(a)(1)(A) deadline. If the bureau misses the deadline, the disputed item must be deleted under §611(a)(5)(A)(i) — verified or not — making missed deadlines a powerful and easily proven violation.
• It creates the rock-solid evidentiary record required for any later litigation. The combination of (a) the dated dispute letter, (b) the certified-mail tracking number, (c) the signed return receipt, and (d) the bureau's response (or non-response) is Exhibit A in any federal-court complaint under FCRA §616/§617.
• It is the floor of every Stage 1 escalation. You cannot demand the Method of Verification (Letter 2) without first sending a basic dispute. You cannot establish willful noncompliance for a Stage 3 damages demand (Letter 83) without first showing the bureau ignored a properly-drafted basic dispute. This letter is therefore the foundation of the entire library.
how_to_use:
STEP 1: Pull all three of your credit reports — Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion — directly from AnnualCreditReport.com (free, no enrollment, no hard pull). The same item is often reported differently by each bureau, and you must dispute with each bureau separately.
STEP 2: Identify exactly what is wrong. Be specific. "This account is wrong" is too vague and risks a frivolous-dispute dismissal. Pinpoint the specific data point: balance, status, payment-history grid for a specific month, date opened, date of first delinquency, account number, or account ownership.
STEP 3: Gather your supporting evidence: bank statements, cleared checks, payoff letters, billing statements, court records, identity-theft reports, prior bureau responses, or anything else that documents the inaccuracy. You will reference and enclose this evidence with the letter.
STEP 4: Fill in every [BRACKETED] placeholder in the template below with your exact details. Use your full legal name as it appears on your government-issued ID. Use a current physical mailing address (not a P.O. box) so the bureau cannot reject the dispute on identity grounds.
STEP 5: In the REASON FOR DISPUTE section, write a brief, factual narrative of what is wrong and why. Do not editorialize. State facts only. "This account shows a 30-day late payment for March 2023. I paid the March 2023 statement on March 14, 2023 — well within the grace period. The enclosed bank statement shows the payment cleared on March 16, 2023." That level of specificity defeats a frivolous-dispute claim.
STEP 6: Print the finalized letter on plain white 8.5x11 paper. Sign in blue or black ink. Date the letter the same day you mail it. Make at least two complete copies — one for your file and one for your evidence binder.
STEP 7: Mail via USPS Certified Mail with Return Receipt Requested (the green card). Use a separate envelope for each bureau and send to all three at the bureau-specific consumer-dispute addresses listed below. Save the certified-mail receipt and retain the green card when it returns. The certified-mail tracking number is your evidentiary anchor for the entire investigation.
STEP 8: Wait thirty (30) days from the bureau's receipt date (the signed return-receipt date, NOT the postmark date). The bureau's investigation results must be mailed to you within that window per FCRA §611(a)(1)(A) and §611(a)(6).
STEP 9: Read the bureau's response carefully when it arrives. There are three outcomes. (a) The bureau deletes the item — you are done; pull a fresh report 30-45 days later to confirm the deletion stuck. (b) The bureau modifies the item to your satisfaction — verify the modification is accurate and move on. (c) The bureau "verifies" the item without removing or correcting it — escalate to Letter 2 (Method of Verification Request) immediately.
STEP 10: If thirty days pass with no response, the bureau has missed its statutory deadline under FCRA §611(a)(1)(A). Send Letter 48 (Notice of Stalled Investigation) and demand immediate deletion under §611(a)(5)(A)(i).

MAILING:
Send certified mail with return receipt requested. Use a separate envelope for each bureau and send to ALL THREE so the dispute is on file at every bureau:

  Equifax Information Services LLC
  P.O. Box 740256
  Atlanta, GA 30374-0256

  Experian
  P.O. Box 4500
  Allen, TX 75013

  TransUnion Consumer Solutions
  P.O. Box 2000
  Chester, PA 19016-2000

Keep the certified-mail receipt and the returned green card permanently. The signed receipt is the date that starts the bureau's 30-day FCRA §611 investigation clock.

TIMING:
Send immediately upon discovering the inaccuracy. There is no benefit to waiting. The sooner you send it, the sooner the 30-day clock starts and the sooner the inaccurate item can be removed.
template_body:
[Your Full Legal Name]
[Your Address]
[City, State ZIP]
[Your Email Address]
[Your Phone Number]

[Date]

[Bureau Name - Equifax, Experian, or TransUnion]
Attn: Consumer Dispute Department
[Bureau Address]
[City, State ZIP]

VIA U.S. CERTIFIED MAIL, RETURN RECEIPT REQUESTED
Tracking Number: [Certified Mail Tracking Number]

Re:  FORMAL DISPUTE OF INACCURATE INFORMATION ON CREDIT REPORT
      Pursuant to Fair Credit Reporting Act § 611, 15 U.S.C. § 1681i

Consumer Name: [Your Full Legal Name]
Date of Birth: [MM/DD/YYYY]
Social Security Number: XXX-XX-[Last 4 Digits of SSN]
Current Address: [Your Address], [City, State ZIP]
Previous Address (within last 2 years, if any): [Previous Address]

Dear [Bureau Name - Equifax, Experian, or TransUnion]:

I am writing to formally dispute inaccurate information appearing on the consumer credit file your agency maintains under my name. Pursuant to Section 611 of the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA), 15 U.S.C. § 1681i, I demand a prompt and reasonable reinvestigation of the item identified below and correction or deletion of the inaccurate information within the thirty (30) day period required by 15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(1)(A).

I.  ITEM IN DISPUTE

Account Name (Furnisher): [Creditor Name]
Account Number: [Account Number - or last 4 digits]
Account Type: [Account Type - Credit Card / Installment Loan / Mortgage / Auto Loan / Collection / Other]
Reported Balance: [Reported Balance]
Reported Status: [Status Shown on Report]
Reported Date Opened: [Date Opened on Report]
Reported Date of Last Activity / Date of First Delinquency: [Dates as reported]

II.  REASON FOR DISPUTE

The information your agency is publishing regarding this account is factually incorrect. Specifically:

[Insert a concise factual narrative of the inaccuracy. Examples — pick the one that fits and edit for your facts:
  - "This account shows a 30-day late payment for [Month/Year]. I paid the [Month/Year] statement in full and on time. The enclosed bank statement shows the payment cleared on [Exact Date]. The reported late mark is therefore false."
  - "This account is reporting an outstanding balance of $[Amount]. I paid this account in full on [Exact Date], and the enclosed payoff letter from the creditor confirms a $0.00 balance. The reported balance is therefore false."
  - "This account is reporting a charge-off status. I was never 180 days delinquent on this account, which is the legal and accounting prerequisite for a charge-off. The reported status is therefore false."
  - "This account is reporting a Date of First Delinquency of [Month/Year]. The actual Date of First Delinquency was [Earlier Month/Year]. Under 15 U.S.C. § 1681c(c), the DoFD is fixed and cannot be re-aged. The reported date is therefore false."
  - "This account does not belong to me. I have no contractual relationship with [Creditor Name] and have never opened this account."]

III.  STATUTORY DUTIES YOU MUST PERFORM

Pursuant to FCRA §611 (15 U.S.C. § 1681i) and FCRA §623(b) (15 U.S.C. § 1681s-2(b)), upon receipt of this dispute you must:

1.  Conduct a reasonable reinvestigation of the disputed item. A purely mechanical, automated re-check of the furnisher's electronic record (commonly performed via the e-OSCAR ACDV system) does not, standing alone, satisfy this duty where the consumer has supplied specific factual evidence of inaccuracy.

2.  Forward this dispute and ALL relevant information I have submitted to the furnisher of the disputed information, [Creditor Name], within five (5) business days, as required by 15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(2).

3.  Require the furnisher to perform its own investigation under 15 U.S.C. § 1681s-2(b) — including a review of the documents I have enclosed — and to report the results to your agency.

4.  If the disputed information cannot be verified as accurate after reasonable reinvestigation, DELETE the item from my credit file pursuant to 15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(5)(A)(i). Marking an item "verified" without genuine verification is not an acceptable substitute for deletion.

5.  Provide me with written notice of the results of the reinvestigation within thirty (30) days of your receipt of this dispute, as required by 15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(1)(A) and §1681i(a)(6).

6.  If the disputed item is deleted or modified, transmit the corrected information to every other nationwide consumer reporting agency that received the inaccurate data, as required by 15 U.S.C. § 1681i(a)(6)(B)(iii).

IV.  PRESERVATION OF EVIDENCE AND RIGHTS

I am preserving this dispute, the certified-mail tracking record, and all of your agency's responses for use in any future regulatory or judicial proceedings. I expect your agency to do the same. Any destruction or alteration of records relating to this dispute after your receipt of this letter will be addressed as spoliation in any subsequent litigation.

I do not waive any rights, defenses, or claims I may have against your agency, the furnisher, or any other party. Nothing in this letter is intended as, nor should be construed as, a waiver of my rights under the FCRA or any other applicable law.

V.  CONSEQUENCES OF NONCOMPLIANCE

If your agency fails to conduct a reasonable reinvestigation, fails to delete or correct the disputed information when accuracy cannot be verified, or fails to respond within thirty (30) days, I will treat that failure as either willful noncompliance under FCRA §616 (15 U.S.C. § 1681n) or, at a minimum, negligent noncompliance under FCRA §617 (15 U.S.C. § 1681o). Both sections expose your agency to actual damages, statutory damages of up to $1,000.00 per violation in willful cases, punitive damages where supported by the evidence, and reasonable attorney's fees and costs under the FCRA's fee-shifting provisions. I will additionally file complaints with the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and the Office of the Attorney General for the State of [Your State of Residence].

I look forward to your prompt and thorough compliance with federal law.

Sincerely,

[Your Signature]
[Your Printed Name]

Enclosures:
- Copy of credit report with the disputed item clearly marked
- Copy of state-issued photo identification
- Copy of Social Security card or W-2 confirming SSN
- Copy of current utility bill confirming address
- [Supporting documentation specific to this dispute, e.g., bank statement, payoff letter, prior bureau response, identity-theft report]
